summary_text:
"Wall Street's main indexes edged lower on Tuesday, with the S&P 500 and Nasdaq pulling back from record highs after a hotter-than-expected inflation report and fading hopes for a swift resolution to the Middle East conflict. The Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 307 points, while the Nasdaq fell 0.2% from its all-time high set the day before. Robert Kiyosaki's latest warning pushed fear, curiosity, and debate through global financial markets. The Nasdaq 100 fell over 2% as hot US CPI inflation shook Wall Street. The Bureau of Labor Statistics reported that headline inflation rose to 3.3% from 3.2%, with the index falling to its lowest level since September. A rebound for technology stocks led Wall Street to records Wednesday, even though the majority of stocks fell following another discouraging update on inflation. The S&PM 500 and the NASDAQ will look to bounce back and trade in green on Wednesday even as Wall Street futures were still in red in the pre-market.

The US stock market took a sharp hit on Tuesday as investors faced a troubling wave of economic data. Some investors have raised apprehensions that the rapid uptick in stock prices is similar to that of the 1990s dot-com bubble, raising fears that the next downturn could be as sharp as the one we saw in 2000. As investors weighed cooling tech momentum against rising energy prices and fresh inflation data, the market ended on a mixed note, with a combination of hot CPI data, cooling Mideast conflict hopes, and rising oil prices adding to the bearish sentiment. A sudden halt for tech stocks put the brakes on Wall Street's record-setting run, though a rebound for tech led to new highs. Investors are now looking ahead to Thursday's jobs data and Fed policy signals for clues on whether the economy can avoid a hard landing. The coming days will likely determine whether the market can regain its footing or whether the worst of the downturn is behind us."
